title: Culture-Aware Humorous Captioning: Multimodal Humor Generation across Cultural Contexts
research_questions: Can multimodal large language models generate short humorous captions that remain grounded in an input image while adapting to an explicitly specified cultural context?
How can open-ended, culture-conditioned humorous image captions be evaluated across image grounding, cultural appropriateness, semantic quality, coherence, humor, and creativity?

Does a staged alignment framework combining Western-context supervised fine-tuning, judge-based GRPO, degradation-aware regularization, and low-resource Eastern adaptation improve contextual fit and overall caption quality?
Does explicit cultural context conditioning improve performance compared with no cultural context conditioning?

datasets_used.name: culture-aware humorous captioning training pool
datasets_used.size: 3,500 images; Stage 1 uses 3,000 image-text samples; Stage 3 uses 1,000 image-text pairs

methods_proposed.name: CuHAlign
methods_proposed.summary: A three-stage alignment framework for culture-aware humorous captioning. It first performs Western-context supervised fine-tuning on Qwen3-VL-8B with LoRA, then applies judge-based GRPO with multi-dimensional preference rankings, and finally adapts to Eastern cultural context using a small supervised mixture with Western replay.

methods_proposed.name: Degradation-aware Prototype Repulsion Constraint
methods_proposed.summary: A constraint added during preference optimization to mitigate reward hacking in open-ended generation. It builds prototype vectors for degradation directions from annotated evidence texts and penalizes generated outputs whose representation is too similar to the corresponding degradation prototype.

methods_proposed.name: Six-dimensional evaluation framework
methods_proposed.summary: An evaluation rubric scoring Image Relevance, Contextual Fit, Semantic Richness, Reasonableness, Humor, and Creativity on 10-point scales. The paper reports each dimension and their unweighted average.

evaluation_methods.detail: A fixed 20% subset of the 1,000 test samples is scored by human annotators using the six-dimensional 10-point rubric.

The remaining 80% of the 1,000 test samples is scored automatically by an LLM-as-a-Judge under the same unified rubric, and final results average human-scored and judge-scored portions over the full test set.

For preference alignment, the current policy samples K = 8 rollouts per training sample; the rollouts plus a reference caption are ranked by an LLM judge, and rewards are constructed from each rollout's rank relative to the reference.
A judge validation set of 400 image instances is built as image + high-quality caption + low-quality caption pairs with manually predefined better-worse labels.
GPT-5.1, Gemini-3-Flash-Preview, and Claude Sonnet 4.5 are evaluated as pairwise judges on the 400-instance validation set; GPT-5.1 has the highest agreement in all four language/difficulty settings.

headline_results.finding: CuHAlign achieved the highest Contextual Fit under the Western cultural context, improving over the Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct backbone from 5.49 to 6.63.
CuHAlign achieved the highest Contextual Fit under the Eastern cultural context, improving over the Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct backbone from 5.22 to 6.30.
On overall average in Table 2, GPT-4o scored 6.76 under Western context and 6.75 under Eastern context, compared with CuHAlign's 6.63 and 6.66.
Explicit cultural conditioning increased CuHAlign's Contextual Fit from 5.21 with no context to 6.63 under Western context and 6.30 under Eastern context.
In the ablation, Western-context CF rose from 5.49 for the base model to 6.13 after SFT, 6.59 after GRPO, 6.62 after the degradation constraint, and 6.63 for the full model.
GPT-5.1 had the highest judge-human agreement across all judge-validation settings: 68.2, 77.3, 71.6, and 79.2.

key_findings: Contextual Fit separates culture-aware humorous captioning from general humorous captioning: model gaps are larger on CF than on several general caption-quality dimensions.
CuHAlign obtains the best CF under both cultural settings while maintaining competitive overall scores, but GPT-4o has the highest overall averages in Table 2.
Explicit cultural context substantially improves CF for both Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct and CuHAlign compared with the no-context setting.

The staged alignment ablation shows SFT establishes task behavior, GRPO is the main step for improving generation quality, the degradation-aware constraint adds a smaller balance improvement, and Eastern adaptation is crucial for the low-resource Eastern setting.
Judge validation shows automatic evaluation is more reliable for clear quality gaps and harder for fine-grained ambiguous pairs; GPT-5.1 performs best among the three judges tested.
limitations: The paper states that the Chinese–Eastern and English–Western setup may not fully disentangle language effects from cultural effects.
The task uses only two coarse-grained cultural conditions, Eastern and Western, and the paper states this is not a fine-grained or exhaustive definition of culture.
Benchmark references are intended as stable high-quality anchors and are not intended to exhaust all valid humorous expressions.
Automatic judge evaluation is reliable for obvious quality gaps but remains challenging for fine-grained discrimination on ambiguous pairs.
